Body:
"section" has been misspelled as "secion" in a lot of places (just grep for it :))

https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/3d3ffbe6e15fc7e4d8a4d275e9fb3a334e31c8a3/Projects/STM32H750B-DK/Examples/MDMA/MDMA_LinkedList/SW4STM32/STM32H750B-DISCO/STM32H750XBHx_FLASH.ld#L142

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-07-27T11:29:57Z]
Duplicate of #37.
issue_title: Spelling: "secion" instead of "section" in >900 places

title: [H7] #34: Dual Core boot up example
content:
Issue #34: Dual Core boot up example Title: Dual Core boot up example Body: **Describe the set-up** * STM32H757I-EVAL * STM32CubeIDE 1.3.1 **Additional context** I'm following the templates for the Dual Core bootup examples, when both cores boot at the same time (OB set accordingly, both checked). The CPU2 boots-up, gets into Stop mode. I can't see how it gets out of Stop mode. I can see that CPU1 releases HSEM 0 but is this producing an event? There are no interrupts generated and checking the documentation I can't see HSEM generating events. I can't make the code to work. Is the example correct? Comments: [ALABSTM - 2020-04-29T14:18:08Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, Thank you for this report. It may be related to this issue described in pull-request #26. Within your [system_stm32h7xx_dualcore_boot_cm4_cm7.c](https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Drivers/CMSIS/Device/ST/STM32H7xx/Source/Templates/system_stm32h7xx_dualcore_boot_cm4_cm7.c) file, update the line indicated below: diff /* Configure the Vector Table location add offset address ------------------*/ #ifdef VECT_TAB_SRAM - SCB->VTOR = D2_AHBSRAM_BASE | VECT_TAB_OFFSET; /* Vector Table Relocation in Internal SRAM */ + SCB->VTOR = D2_AXISRAM_BASE | VECT_TAB_OFFSET; /* Vector Table Relocation in Internal SRAM */ #else SCB->VTOR = FLASH_BANK2_BASE | VECT_TAB_OFFSET; /* Vector Table Relocation in Internal FLASH */ #endif Would you mind trying this solution and reporting whether it solved the problem or not? Thank you. With regards, [ALABSTM - 2020-05-04T10:24:40Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, Any feedback about this issue? Thank you, [bytesontherocks - 2020-05-04T10:56:30Z] Hi @ALABSTM, I haven't been able to test it yet but I was not using NVIC from RAM, so I don't think is this issue. I will re-run some of the test I did with some change in the eval board and I'll report. Thanks [ALABSTM - 2020-05-04T13:00:25Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, Thank you for your reply. I will wait for your updated report. With regards, [bytesontherocks - 2020-05-05T15:39:55Z] Hi @ALABSTM, Imported a fresh template of BootCM4CM7 project from SW4STM32 to STM32CubeIDE (1.3.1). I set up the project the following way: - M7 debug session flashes both images - M4 debug session attaches to the system instead of start a debug session To have some visual feedback I added for each core a different blinking LED. M7 debug session started correctly but it goes to error_handler. The issue is mentioned here: https://visualgdb.com/tutorials/arm/stm32/multicore/startup/ so I replace the while loop with the timeout with while(__HAL_RCC_GET_FLAG(RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY) != RESET) { asm("NOP");} That didn't improve it, the M7 gets stuck waiting at RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY, while the M4 can not attach (STLink doesn't connect) but starting a new debug session for the M4 without reseting the core connects but gets stuck in reset handler. To rule out that is not my debug settings what are causing this situation I programmed both cores with STM32CubeProgrammer, no LEDS flashing either. I removed the HSEM and initial RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY checks in the M7 and the HSEM and going to Stop from M4: Both LEDS blinking and I am able to debug both cores. Both OB BM4 and BCM7 are set. I think there are some issues with waking up from the Stop mode in the M4. Are you able to tell me if I'm doing something wrong or is the code that is not fully set up? [ALABSTM - 2020-05-06T13:58:17Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, Thank you for this detailed report and for the explanatory link you provided. Your report will be forwarded to our development teams to be analysed. I will be back to you as soon as I get their feedback. But allow me question before: do you confirm you are using the latest firmware published (i.e. STM32CubeH7 **v1.7.0**)? Thank you. With regards, [bytesontherocks - 2020-05-06T14:07:08Z] Hi @ALABSTM ,Yes I do, I cloned the repo from this github location and I double checked it is up to date v1.7.0. Looking forward to the feedback. Thanks. [ALABSTM - 2020-05-14T07:53:43Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, I hope you are doing well. In the [BootCM4_CM7](https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/tree/master/Projects/STM32H747I-EVAL/Templates/BootCM4_CM7) example, there are two confusingly similar while() loops implemented from the CM7 side: * First one waiting for the CM4 to **enter** the "stop" mode. https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/79196b09acfb720589f58e93ccf956401b18a191/Projects/STM32H747I-EVAL/Templates/BootCM4_CM7/CM7/Src/main.c#L62 * Second one waiting for the CM4 to **wake up from** the "stop" mode. https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/79196b09acfb720589f58e93ccf956401b18a191/Projects/STM32H747I-EVAL/Templates/BootCM4_CM7/CM7/Src/main.c#L95 According to your comment, you set the the asm("nop"); instruction within the first loop. However, would it be possible that actually you **inadvertently** set it within the second one? Please excuse the question. It is simply meant to remove this possibility from the list of potential causes of the issue you are facing. Thank you, [bytesontherocks - 2020-05-14T09:55:03Z] HI @ALABSTM, I tried the following options for the loops you mentioned: - with ErrorHandler() + timeout as per the example - with asm("nop"); in both while loops that you have indicated. Neither worked, neither debugging or not. Replying directly to your question, yes, I placed asm("nop"); to Line 95 but not inadvertently, I thought that was the right thing to do when debugging. The implementation of the ErrorHandler() in the BootCM4_CM7 example is a while(1). The difference between executing asm("nop") you don't give up and keep checking the RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY and with the ErrorHandler and timeout the CM7 core does nothing else, Is there some timing effect that I should be aware of? Thanks. [ALABSTM - 2020-05-26T11:19:59Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, Please have a look to [this](https://www.st.com/resource/en/application_note/dm00629855-getting-started-with-projects-based-on-dualcore-stm32h7-microcontrollers-in-stm32cubeide-stmicroelectronics.pdf) application note available [here](https://www.st.com/en/development-tools/stm32cubeide.html#) (scroll down to "APPLICATION NOTES" section) on our st.com website. Chapter 3 at page 12 explains how to debug dual core applications using anyone of the two debuggers (STLINK GDB server or OpenOCD) offered by Cube IDE. I hope this will help you overcome the issue you are facing. Please let me know if this is the case or if you still need help. With regards, [bytesontherocks - 2020-05-27T20:38:05Z] Hi @ALABSTM, Thanks for your reply. I followed the document you have mentioned when I was performing the tests. Also, as I reported previously, to make sure it was not due to debugging settings, I tried to execute the binaries flashing them with STCubeProgrammer, with same results. I assume you (somebody in ST) has tested this example/template in the mentioned eval board, could you provide the Option Bytes values that you use to test it, please? [ALABSTM - 2020-06-12T21:58:39Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, According to our development teams, the project structure of multi-cpu examples has significantly evolved in Cube IDE compared to SW4STM32. Below is a solution they suggest you the solution below hoping it will definitely solve the problem you are facing. Regarding the options bits, in case you have not modified the default values there shall be no issue from that side. However, in case you always want to have the configuration used, do not hesitate. The solution is to create a new project from scratch using the Cube IDE. Here are the steps to follow: * Create a new H7 DC (Dual Core) project based on the HAL. * In the main() function of the CM7 sub-project, modify the **first** while loop as you already did. diff -while((__HAL_RCC_GET_FLAG(RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY) != RESET) && (timeout-- > 0)); -if (timeout < 0) { Error_Handler(); } +while(__HAL_RCC_GET_FLAG(RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY) != RESET) { asm("NOP"); } * Build both CM7 and CM4 sub-projects. * Create a **first** debug configuration for the **CM7** sub-project using the **ST-Link GDB Server** probe. * From the startup tab, load **both** binaries into the flash memory. * Screenshots: * Startup tab. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84530219-ac319500-acda-11ea-945d-47da3edd39f5.png) * Debugger tab. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84528610-ed747580-acd7-11ea-83c1-f147cc8e7bf1.png) * Create a **second** debug configuration for the **CM4** sub-project using the **ST-Link GDB Server** probe. * From the startup tab, **do not** load CM4-related binary into the flash memory. * From the debugger tab, perform also an **Autostart** of the server, but pay **attention** to: * Increment the GDB port number (+2 for example). * Set the reset type to **none**. * Screenshots: * Startup tab. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84529418-56a8b880-acd9-11ea-819c-2691b0df978d.png) * Debugger tab. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84529994-4c3aee80-acda-11ea-80b9-f9e0555990c0.png) * Run the CM7 core. * Code execution should break at the level of the first instruction, **i.e.** the modified while loop. * Run the CM4 core. * Code execution should break at the level of the first instruction, **i.e.** __HAL_RCC_HSEM_CLK_ENABLE();. * Set a break point on the HAL_Init(); instruction. This will serve later. * So far, this is how your Cube IDE window should look like. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84547422-6df89d80-acfb-11ea-80e2-6fccf09427ca.png) * Resume the CM4 code execution. * It shall enter the sleep mode. * On the CM4 sub-project window, you should read that execution broke at address **0xa05f0000**. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84547857-40f8ba80-acfc-11ea-896b-bba6985f61d7.png) * Resume the CM7 code execution. * The CM7 core will wake the CM4. * The GDB client is not aware that the CM4 core woke up. Nothing particular will be displayed on the CM4 sub-project window. * Now, simply click on the "suspend/resume" button of the CM4 sub-project. * The window should display the C code again. * Code execution should halt at the level of the HAL_Init(); instruction (thanks to the beak point we set earlier). ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84548318-3ee32b80-acfd-11ea-8f74-778a56ebe11e.png) * Now you can debug execution on both cores as you wish. I hope this tutorial will finally help you enjoy this dual core example. Please do not hesitate in case you still face some issue. With regards, [bytesontherocks - 2020-06-24T20:42:46Z] Hi, Thanks for your instructions. I followed them with this result: -debug M7 and waits into the loop as expected -launch debug or run M4 and the following error appears: Error in final launch sequence: Failed to start GDB server Failed to start GDB server Error in initializing ST-LINK device. Reason: (18) Could not verify ST device! Abort connection. This is a genuine H7 eval board, using STLink v3 onboard. Any clue where is the problem? Thanks. ![Launc_M4error](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44874065/85625496-776ef780-b663-11ea-9862-5ad9afa5b40b.JPG) [ALABSTM - 2020-07-22T14:38:28Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, Please excuse my delayed reply. I will forward this to our development teams hoping they will help you definitely solve the problem. Once again, excuse this delayed reply and thank you for your patience. With regards, [ALABSTM - 2020-07-29T12:40:29Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, I have feedback from our development teams: * They asked whether you checked the "Halt all cores" option in the "Debugger" tab of the **CM7** sub-project (bottom of the screenshot below). * In case you did, however, but still get the error message, they suggested the following test: * From the **CM7** debugging session: * Once you obtain the error message you mentioned... * ... resume code execution again to **wake up** the CM4 core. * From the **CM4** debugging session: * Reconnect to the **CM4** core (by simply restarting the debugging session for example). * Resume code execution. * Code execution should halt at the level of the HAL_Init(); instruction (if a beak point is still set there). We are looking forward to reading your feedback to know whether it is a simple foresight to activate the "Halt all cores" option or, in the contrary case, whether the **CM4** core execution resumed normally after it has been woken up by the **CM7** core. With regards,

[IMAGE ATTACHED]

[bytesontherocks - 2020-08-06T12:37:39Z] hi @ALABSTM, Thanks for your reply. After following your instructions I kept having issues. Finally I started having a look at the Option Bytes (this is why some posts ago I asked about the OB settings but I suppose it was forgotten about). NRST_STOP_D2 and NRST_STBY_D2 were unchecked and that was preventing to M4 to resume, so I checked them both, even if NRST_STOP_D2 sould be the one to check for this particular issue. I would recommend you to include the OB settings in your documentation on how to debug Dual Core. I close the issue and thanks again. [IMAGE ATTACHED] [ALABSTM - 2020-08-07T10:06:44Z] Hi @bytesontherocks, Really glad to know you overcame this problem. Thank you for all the details you provided. I will let our development teams know about this OB-related aspect and about your suggestion. Thank you once more for your contribution. With regards,
paragraphs: Issue #34: Dual Core boot up example Title: Dual Core boot up example

Body:
**Describe the set-up**
 * STM32H757I-EVAL
 * STM32CubeIDE 1.3.1

**Additional context**
I'm following the templates for the Dual Core bootup examples, when both cores boot at the same time (OB set accordingly, both checked).

The CPU2 boots-up, gets into Stop mode. I can't see how it gets out of Stop mode. I can see that CPU1 releases HSEM 0 but is this producing an event? There are no interrupts generated and checking the documentation I can't see HSEM generating events.
I can't make the code to work. Is the example correct?

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-04-29T14:18:08Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,

Thank you for this report. It may be related to this issue described in pull-request #26. Within your [system_stm32h7xx_dualcore_boot_cm4_cm7.c](https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Drivers/CMSIS/Device/ST/STM32H7xx/Source/Templates/system_stm32h7xx_dualcore_boot_cm4_cm7.c) file, update the line indicated below:
diff
  /* Configure the Vector Table location add offset address ------------------*/
#ifdef VECT_TAB_SRAM
-  SCB->VTOR = D2_AHBSRAM_BASE | VECT_TAB_OFFSET; /* Vector Table Relocation in Internal SRAM */
+  SCB->VTOR = D2_AXISRAM_BASE | VECT_TAB_OFFSET; /* Vector Table Relocation in Internal SRAM */
#else
  SCB->VTOR = FLASH_BANK2_BASE | VECT_TAB_OFFSET; /* Vector Table Relocation in Internal FLASH */
#endif
Would you mind trying this solution and reporting whether it solved the problem or not? Thank you.

With regards, [ALABSTM - 2020-05-04T10:24:40Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,
Any feedback about this issue? Thank you,

[bytesontherocks - 2020-05-04T10:56:30Z]
Hi @ALABSTM, 
I haven't been able to test it yet but I was not using NVIC from RAM, so I don't think is this issue. I will re-run some of the test I did with some change in the eval board and I'll report. 
Thanks

[ALABSTM - 2020-05-04T13:00:25Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,
Thank you for your reply. I will wait for your updated report. With regards,

[bytesontherocks - 2020-05-05T15:39:55Z]
Hi @ALABSTM,
Imported a fresh template of BootCM4CM7 project from SW4STM32 to STM32CubeIDE (1.3.1). I set up the project the following way: 
- M7 debug session flashes both images 
- M4 debug session attaches to the system instead of start a debug session
To have some visual feedback I added for each core a different blinking LED.
M7 debug session started correctly but it goes to error_handler. The issue is mentioned here: https://visualgdb.com/tutorials/arm/stm32/multicore/startup/
so I replace the while loop with the timeout with while(__HAL_RCC_GET_FLAG(RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY) != RESET) { asm("NOP");}

That didn't improve it, the M7 gets stuck waiting at RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY, while the M4 can not attach (STLink doesn't connect) but starting a new debug session for the M4 without reseting the core connects but gets stuck in reset handler.
To rule out that is not my debug settings what are causing this situation I programmed both cores with STM32CubeProgrammer, no LEDS flashing either.
I removed the HSEM and initial RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY checks in the M7 and the HSEM and going to Stop from M4: Both LEDS blinking and I am able to debug both cores.
I think there are some issues with waking up from the Stop mode in the M4.
Are you able to tell me if I'm doing something wrong or is the code that is not fully set up?

[ALABSTM - 2020-05-06T13:58:17Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,

Thank you for this detailed report and for the explanatory link you provided. Your report will be forwarded to our development teams to be analysed. I will be back to you as soon as I get their feedback.
But allow me question before: do you confirm you are using the latest firmware published (i.e. STM32CubeH7 **v1.7.0**)? Thank you.

[bytesontherocks - 2020-05-06T14:07:08Z]
Hi @ALABSTM ,Yes I do, I cloned the repo from this github location and I double checked it is up to date v1.7.0.
Looking forward to the feedback. Thanks.

[ALABSTM - 2020-05-14T07:53:43Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,

I hope you are doing well. In the [BootCM4_CM7](https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/tree/master/Projects/STM32H747I-EVAL/Templates/BootCM4_CM7) example, there are two confusingly similar while() loops implemented from the CM7 side:
* First one waiting for the CM4 to **enter** the "stop" mode.
https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/79196b09acfb720589f58e93ccf956401b18a191/Projects/STM32H747I-EVAL/Templates/BootCM4_CM7/CM7/Src/main.c#L62
* Second one waiting for the CM4 to **wake up from** the "stop" mode.
https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/79196b09acfb720589f58e93ccf956401b18a191/Projects/STM32H747I-EVAL/Templates/BootCM4_CM7/CM7/Src/main.c#L95
According to your comment, you set the the asm("nop"); instruction within the first loop. However, would it be possible that actually you **inadvertently** set it within the second one?
Please excuse the question. It is simply meant to remove this possibility from the list of potential causes of the issue you are facing.

[bytesontherocks - 2020-05-14T09:55:03Z]
HI @ALABSTM,
I tried the following options for the loops you mentioned:
- with ErrorHandler() + timeout as per the example 
- with asm("nop"); in both while loops that you have indicated.
Neither worked, neither debugging or not.
Replying directly to your question, yes, I placed asm("nop"); to Line 95 but not inadvertently, I thought that was the right thing to do when debugging.

The implementation of the ErrorHandler() in the BootCM4_CM7 example is a while(1). The difference between executing asm("nop") you don't give up and keep checking the RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY and with the ErrorHandler and timeout the CM7 core does nothing else, Is there some timing effect that I should be aware of?

Thanks. [ALABSTM - 2020-05-26T11:19:59Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,

Please have a look to [this](https://www.st.com/resource/en/application_note/dm00629855-getting-started-with-projects-based-on-dualcore-stm32h7-microcontrollers-in-stm32cubeide-stmicroelectronics.pdf) application note available [here](https://www.st.com/en/development-tools/stm32cubeide.html#) (scroll down to "APPLICATION NOTES" section) on our st.com website.
Chapter 3 at page 12 explains how to debug dual core applications using anyone of the two debuggers (STLINK GDB server or OpenOCD) offered by Cube IDE.
I hope this will help you overcome the issue you are facing. Please let me know if this is the case or if you still need help.

[bytesontherocks - 2020-05-27T20:38:05Z]
Hi @ALABSTM, 
Thanks for your reply. I followed the document you have mentioned when I was performing the tests. Also, as I reported previously, to make sure it was not due to debugging settings, I tried to execute the binaries flashing them with STCubeProgrammer, with same results.
I assume you (somebody in ST) has tested this example/template in the mentioned eval board, could you provide the Option Bytes values that you use to test it, please?

[ALABSTM - 2020-06-12T21:58:39Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,

According to our development teams, the project structure of multi-cpu examples has significantly evolved in  Cube IDE compared to SW4STM32. Below is a solution they suggest you the solution below hoping it will definitely solve the problem you are facing.
Regarding the options bits, in case you have not modified the default values there shall be no issue from that side. However, in case you always want to have the configuration used, do not hesitate.

The solution is to create a new project from scratch using the Cube IDE. Here are the steps to follow:
* Create a new H7 DC (Dual Core) project based on the HAL.
* In the main() function of the CM7 sub-project, modify the **first** while loop as you already did.
  diff
  -while((__HAL_RCC_GET_FLAG(RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY) != RESET) && (timeout-- > 0));
  -if (timeout < 0) { Error_Handler(); }
  +while(__HAL_RCC_GET_FLAG(RCC_FLAG_D2CKRDY) != RESET) { asm("NOP"); }
  
* Build both CM7 and CM4 sub-projects.
* Create a **first** debug configuration for the **CM7** sub-project using the **ST-Link GDB Server** probe.
  * From the startup tab, load **both** binaries into the flash memory.
  * Screenshots:
    * Startup tab.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84530219-ac319500-acda-11ea-945d-47da3edd39f5.png)
    * Debugger tab.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84528610-ed747580-acd7-11ea-83c1-f147cc8e7bf1.png)
* Create a **second** debug configuration for the **CM4** sub-project using the **ST-Link GDB Server** probe.
  * From the startup tab, **do not** load CM4-related binary into the flash memory.
  * From the debugger tab, perform also an **Autostart** of the server, but pay **attention** to:
    * Increment the GDB port number (+2 for example).
    * Set the reset type to **none**.
  * Screenshots:
    * Startup tab.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84529418-56a8b880-acd9-11ea-819c-2691b0df978d.png)
    * Debugger tab.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84529994-4c3aee80-acda-11ea-80b9-f9e0555990c0.png)
* Run the CM7 core.
  * Code execution should break at the level of the first instruction, **i.e.** the modified while loop.
* Run the CM4 core.
  * Code execution should break at the level of the first instruction, **i.e.** __HAL_RCC_HSEM_CLK_ENABLE();.
  * Set a break point on the HAL_Init(); instruction. This will serve later.
  * So far, this is how your Cube IDE window should look like.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84547422-6df89d80-acfb-11ea-80e2-6fccf09427ca.png)
* Resume the CM4 code execution.
  * It shall enter the sleep mode.
  * On the CM4 sub-project window, you should read that execution broke at address **0xa05f0000**.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84547857-40f8ba80-acfc-11ea-896b-bba6985f61d7.png)
* Resume the CM7 code execution.
  * The CM7 core will wake the CM4.
  * The GDB client is not aware that the CM4 core woke up. Nothing particular will be displayed on the CM4 sub-project window.
* Now, simply click on the "suspend/resume" button of the CM4 sub-project.
  * The window should display the C code again.
  * Code execution should halt at the level of the HAL_Init(); instruction (thanks to the beak point we set earlier).
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46019491/84548318-3ee32b80-acfd-11ea-8f74-778a56ebe11e.png)
* Now you can debug execution on both cores as you wish.
I hope this tutorial will finally help you enjoy this dual core example. Please do not hesitate in case you still face some issue.

[bytesontherocks - 2020-06-24T20:42:46Z]
Hi, 
Thanks for your instructions.

I followed them with this result:
-debug M7 and waits into the loop as expected
-launch debug or run M4 and the following error appears:

Failed to start GDB server
Failed to start GDB server
Error in initializing ST-LINK device.
Reason: (18) Could not verify ST device! Abort connection.
This is a genuine H7 eval board, using STLink v3 onboard. Any clue where is the problem?
![Launc_M4error](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44874065/85625496-776ef780-b663-11ea-9862-5ad9afa5b40b.JPG)

[ALABSTM - 2020-07-22T14:38:28Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,
Please excuse my delayed reply. I will forward this to our development teams hoping they will help you definitely solve the problem.
Once again, excuse this delayed reply and thank you for your patience.

With regards, [ALABSTM - 2020-07-29T12:40:29Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,

I have feedback from our development teams:
* They asked whether you checked the "Halt all cores" option in the "Debugger" tab of the **CM7** sub-project (bottom of the screenshot below).
* In case you did, however, but still get the error message, they suggested the following test:
  * From the **CM7** debugging session:
    * Once you obtain the error message you mentioned...
    * ... resume code execution again to **wake up** the CM4 core.
  * From the **CM4** debugging session:
    * Reconnect to the **CM4** core (by simply restarting the debugging session for example).
    * Resume code execution.
    * Code execution should halt at the level of the HAL_Init(); instruction (if a beak point is still set there).

We are looking forward to reading your feedback to know whether it is a simple foresight to activate the "Halt all cores" option or, in the contrary case, whether the **CM4** core execution resumed normally after it has been woken up by the **CM7** core.

With regards,

[IMAGE ATTACHED]

[bytesontherocks - 2020-08-06T12:37:39Z]
hi @ALABSTM,

Thanks for your reply. After following your instructions I kept having issues. Finally I started having a look at the Option Bytes (this is why some posts ago I asked about the OB settings but I suppose it was forgotten about). NRST_STOP_D2 and NRST_STBY_D2 were unchecked and that was preventing to M4 to resume, so I checked them both, even if NRST_STOP_D2 sould be the one to check for this particular issue.
I would recommend you to include the OB settings in your documentation on how to debug Dual Core.
I close the issue and thanks again. [IMAGE ATTACHED]

[ALABSTM - 2020-08-07T10:06:44Z]
Hi @bytesontherocks,

Really glad to know you overcame this problem. Thank you for all the details you provided. I will let our development teams know about this OB-related aspect and about your suggestion. Thank you once more for your contribution.

title: [H7] #33: CubeMX: 0-Pointer access in generated MX_ETH_Init code
paragraphs: Issue #33: CubeMX: 0-Pointer access in generated MX_ETH_Init code

Title: CubeMX: 0-Pointer access in generated MX_ETH_Init code Body:
Hi!
I don't know where to report CubeMX bugs, so I leave it here. It might also be considered a HAL problem.
I noticed this behavior after enabling a MPU region to catch 0-pointer accesses.
But heth.Init.MACAddr is defined as a uint8_t * here:
https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/79196b09acfb720589f58e93ccf956401b18a191/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_hal_eth.h#L366-L382
This causes a 0-Pointer access, when accessing MACAddr[0]. Work-Around:
Initialize heth.Init.MACAddr to point to an array.

c
static void MX_ETH_Init(void)
{ /* USER CODE BEGIN ETH_Init 0 */

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-04-10T14:57:37Z]
Hi @thomask77,
To report issues related to the "Cube MX" tool or ask questions about, there is a dedicated [section](https://community.st.com/s/topic/0TO0X000000BTr8WAG/stm32cubemx) in the ST Community forum.
In the fix you suggested, the array looks to be declared as **local** to the MX_ETH_Init(void). Please check whether this might cause an issue at your application level during run-time.

I hope these few hints are helpful. Anyway, it would be preferable that you submit your "Cube MX" related questions to the the ST Community [link](https://community.st.com/s/topic/0TO0X000000BTr8WAG/stm32cubemx). There you will find people better suited to answer such topics.
As issues should be related to the software published within this repository and not to ecosystem or hardware, please allow me to close this one. Thank you for your comprehension.

[thomask77 - 2020-04-12T12:50:58Z]
Thanks for your answer. I'll post the issue to the CubeMX-Forum then, and post a link to this bug for the explanation.

> In the fix you suggested, the array looks to be declared as local to the MX_ETH_Init(void). Please 
> check whether this might cause an issue at your application level during run-time.
I've declared the array as static, so it's a global variable. Your solution is probably better aligned with the ST coding style though.

- Can you add an assert_param statement in HAL_ETH_Init and check the MAC pointer?
  That way, customers will find the problem when using FULL_ASSERT.

[thomask77 - 2020-04-12T14:32:10Z]
I referenced the issue here:
https://community.st.com/s/question/0D53W000004KWmMSAW/cubemx-0pointer-access-in-generated-mxethinit-code

Hope that's the right place.
Greetings, Thomas

[thomask77 - 2021-05-24T12:37:43Z]
@ALABSTM did you at least add an assert_param() statement like mentioned in https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/issues/33#issuecomment-612609455 ?
Otherwise this issue will go unnoticed (as seen in the faulty auto-generated CubeMX code)

title: [H7] #32: CubeMX: 0-pointer access in generated code if TIM6 is used for HAL tick and DAC is enabled
paragraphs: Issue #32: CubeMX: 0-pointer access in generated code if TIM6 is used for HAL tick and DAC is enabled
Title: CubeMX: 0-pointer access in generated code if TIM6 is used for HAL tick and DAC is enabled
I don't know where to report CubeMX bugs, so I leave it here. It might also be considered a HAL problem.
I noticed this behavior after enabling a MPU region to catch 0-pointer accesses.

If TIM6 is used as the HAL Timebase **and** the DAC is enabled, there may be a 0-Pointer access in HAL_DAC_IRQHandler (called by TIM6_DAC_IRQHandler), **if** a tick IRQ happens before MX_DAC1_Init() is called.
The same bug likely exists in all other HAL and CubeMX versions.
Simple workaround: Use another timer, that has a non-shared IRQ handler.

best regards,
Thomas Comments:
[ASELSTM - 2020-04-09T12:21:04Z]
Hi @thomask77 ,

Thank you for this report. We tried to reproduce the issue you described, but we couldn't notice the problem. May I ask you if you were able to reproduce it, and if so what is the procedure followed to get it ?

According to a first analysis, we noticed that CubeMx initializes by default the DAC1 before initializing the TIMx instances. Thus, MX_DAC1_Init() is always called before MX_TIM6_Init(). Therefore, no TIM interruption is generated before DAC initialization.

Moreover, the code generated by CubeMx remains just an initialization, so, no Timbase interruption is enabled until the user implicitly activates them either by calling the interrupt mode APIs (such as HAL_TIM_Base_Start_IT() ) or by activating the update interruption ( calling __HAL_TIM_ENABLE_IT(htim, TIM_IT_UPDATE) or directly enabling TIM_DIER_UIE bit).
 
Take care and stay safe.

[thomask77 - 2020-04-12T14:30:39Z]
Yes, that's the problem - HAL_TIM_Base_Start_IT() is called!

The call chain is:
- main()
- HAL_Init():
  - https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Src/stm32h7xx_hal.c#L134
- HAL_InitTick() (weak function, overridden by CubxMX with a file derived from a template contained in this repository):
  - https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Src/stm32h7xx_hal_timebase_tim_template.c#L58
- HAL_TIM_Base_Start_IT():
  - https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Src/stm32h7xx_hal_timebase_tim_template.c#L114-L121
This happens before HAL_DAC_Init() is called.
Null pointer access is then triggered here:
HAL_DAC_IRQHandler will use the uninitilized Instance pointer.

Steps to reproduce:
* Start CubeMX 5.6.0
* Select NUCELO-H743ZI in Board Selector
* Init all peripherals to default mode -> Yes (doesn't matter)
* System Core -> SYS -> Timebase Source: TIM6
* Analog -> DAC1, OUT1 mode: Connected to external pin only

Generate code. [ASELSTM - 2020-04-28T15:03:27Z]
Hi @thomask77 ,

Our development teams confirmed that this issue is rather related to CubeMX than to the firmware published within this repository. Unfortunately we don't treat aspect related to CubeMx tool in our GitHub repositories.

Please allow me to redirect you to our ST Community. You can report this issue in the [CubeMX related section](https://community.st.com/s/topic/0TO0X000000BTr8WAG/stm32cubemx). There, you will hopefully find people from our CubeMX development teams who will take in charge your request.

Regarding the point you reported, our development teams suggest you a few fixes to avoid the 0 pointer access in this case : 
 
- Force the instances when declaring the handles as following :

DAC_HandleTypeDef     hdac1 = {.Instance = DAC1}; 
  TIM_HandleTypeDef     htim6 = {.Instance = TIM6};
- Checking each peripheral state within the TIM6_DAC_IRQHandler before calling the corresponding IRQHandler :
Please allow me to close this issue now. Thank you again for your report.

[thomask77 - 2020-04-28T17:10:54Z]
I assume you have an internal process, where the CubeMX team monitors bugs here.
Unfortunately, I can't spend the time to report it on the forum. I've already solved the problem for me, but it would be good for your product if the bug gets fixed for everyone else.
issue_title: CubeMX: 0-pointer access in generated code if TIM6 is used for HAL tick and DAC is enabled

title: [H7] #31: USART Receive timeout interrupt not enabled.
content:
Issue #31: USART Receive timeout interrupt not enabled.

Title: USART Receive timeout interrupt not enabled.

Body:
**Describe the set-up**
Hardware: Proprietary hardware containing STM32H743 and external FLASH and external SDRAM.
IDE: VisualGDB with GCC 9.2.1
 
**Additional context**
Setting up the UART receive timeout involves calling these functions:
HAL_UART_ReceiverTimeout_Config(mHuart, mBitsPerCharacter);
HAL_UART_EnableReceiverTimeout(mHuart);

However, that does not enable the receive timeout **interrupt**.  The timeout detection is enabled, but not the interrupt.  The following line should be added to the 'enable' routine, or placed in a separate call:

SET_BIT(mHuart->Instance->CR1, USART_CR1_RTOIE);

Also, after the receive completes, the interrupt should be disabled.

CLEAR_BIT(mHuart->Instance->CR1, USART_CR1_RTOIE);

**Screenshots**
None

- **Board**: STM32H747I-DISO
- **SDK**: STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.6.0
- **Pin**: PD3(CLK), PC7(DATAIN3)

- **Problem**: Filter->DMA bufer OK. **HAL_DFSDM_FilterRegConvCpltCallback** and **HAL_DFSDM_FilterRegConvHalfCpltCallback** function enter normally, but the data is wrong(always the same number). 
I monitor the DFSDM registers, the **RDATAR** register no change.

#define AUDIO_DFSDMx_LEFT_FILTER        DFSDM1_Filter0

#define AUDIO_DFSDMx_DMAx_RIGHT_REQUEST         DMA_REQUEST_DFSDM1_FLT1

title: [H7] #25: Wrong timer identifiers on some HRTIM functions doc
content:
Issue #25: Wrong timer identifiers on some HRTIM functions doc

Title: Wrong timer identifiers on some HRTIM functions doc

Body:
The HRTIM header contains the following definitions as timer identifiers
c
/** @defgroup HRTIM_Timer_identifier HRTIM Timer identifier
  * @{
  * @brief Constants defining timer identifiers
  */
#define HRTIM_TIMERID_MASTER  (HRTIM_MCR_MCEN)   /*!< Master identifier  */
#define HRTIM_TIMERID_TIMER_A (HRTIM_MCR_TACEN)  /*!< Timer A identifier */
#define HRTIM_TIMERID_TIMER_B (HRTIM_MCR_TBCEN)  /*!< Timer B identifier */
#define HRTIM_TIMERID_TIMER_C (HRTIM_MCR_TCCEN)  /*!< Timer C identifier */
#define HRTIM_TIMERID_TIMER_D (HRTIM_MCR_TDCEN)  /*!< Timer D identifier */
#define HRTIM_TIMERID_TIMER_E (HRTIM_MCR_TECEN)  /*!< Timer E identifier */


Some HRTIM functions (e.g. HAL_HRTIM_WaveformCountStop) incorrectly have those definitions documented as
c
  * @param  Timers Timer counter(s) to stop
  *                   This parameter can be any combination of the following values:
  *                   @arg HRTIM_TIMERID_MASTER
  *                   @arg HRTIM_TIMERID_A
  *                   @arg HRTIM_TIMERID_B
  *                   @arg HRTIM_TIMERID_C
  *                   @arg HRTIM_TIMERID_D
  *                   @arg HRTIM_TIMERID_E


As far as I can tell those don't exist, not even as legacy definitions.

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-03-10T11:59:10Z]
Hi Higaski,

Thank you for having pointed out this issue. Indeed, timer identifiers listed in the header of some HAL functions are not aligned with the actual constant names used.

This will be updated in made available in a future release. Thank you again for your contribution.

With regards,

[ALABSTM - 2020-03-10T15:37:43Z]
ST Internal Reference: 82539

title: [H7] #24: HAL_RTC_Init() looses time
content:
Issue #24: HAL_RTC_Init() looses time

Title: HAL_RTC_Init() looses time

Body:
**Describe the set-up**
I'm using a STM32H743 on a custom board with 32768 Hz LSE crystal and battery-backed up RTC.

**Describe the bug**
Every time HAL_RTC_Init() is called, the RTC looses some sub-seconds.

HAL_RTC_Init should check if the RTC is already initializied (year != 0) and/or matches the desired configuration, and skip unnecessary stopping of the clock. According to the data sheet 45.3.8, the library should also check the INITS flag in the RTC_ISR register.

This is big a problem, because CubeMX5 will emit code that calls HAL_RTC_Init on each reset, without checking if it's already initialized. On each reset, you will loose up to 1s.

**How To Reproduce**
1. Read time with HAL_RTC_GetTime &HAL_RTC_GetDate and print to console
2. Call HAL_RTC_Init
3. Repeat

While doing this, the clock will gradually loose seconds.

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-02-12T11:04:08Z]
Hi Thomask77,

Thank you for this one more contribution. This issue has also been reported by the ST Community. We are already working on it. We will let you updated as soon as there is something new from our development teams.

With regards,

[ALABSTM - 2020-02-12T11:04:56Z]
ST Internal Reference: 126471

[ALABSTM - 2020-03-13T10:41:15Z]
Hi Thomask77,

According to our development teams, the issue is at MX initialization-level not at HAL one. The suggested solution looks like the following:
* At user application-level, RTC initialization shall be conditioned:
diff
+/* Do not re-initialize RTC in case it has already been to avoid loosing seconds at each reset */
+if ((RCC->BDCR & RCC_BDCR_RTCEN) == 0)
+{
  if(HAL_RTC_Init(&RtcHandle) != HAL_OK)
  {
    /* Initialization Error */
    Error_Handler(); 
  }
+}
+else
+{
+  HAL_PWR_EnableBkUpAccess();
+}

* These line of codes shall be added into the main() function of your example or application (as the one below, for instance):
https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/3d3ffbe6e15fc7e4d8a4d275e9fb3a334e31c8a3/Projects/NUCLEO-H743ZI/Examples/RTC/RTC_Alarm/Src/main.c#L95-L99

We hope these details help you solve the issue from your side.

This issue has been reported to the CubeMX team. A fix will be available in a future CubeMX release. Unfortunately, we cannot share a date for the moment.

As this is a **CubeMX-related** issue and not directly related to the firmware published whithin this repository, please allow me to close it. Thank you for your comprehension and thank you again for your contribution.

With regards,

[ALABSTM - 2022-04-13T08:35:06Z]
Hi @thomask77,

After further discussions with our development teams, it has been agreed this point could be handled at driver's level. Hence, I will reopen this issue pending a fix is published.

With regards,

[ALABSTM - 2022-04-13T09:00:30Z]
Hi @thomask77,

As for the fix, it will consist in checking the RTC_ISR_INITS bit, which, as described in the reference manual:
* is equal to 0 if the calendar has **not** been initialized
* is equal to 1 if the calendar has been initialized

Thus, the fix should be implemented in hal_rtc.c rather than in main.c and would look something like this:
diff
HAL_StatusTypeDef HAL_RTC_Init(RTC_HandleTypeDef *hrtc)
{
  ...
+    /* Check whether the calendar needs to be initialized */
+    if (RTC_ISR_INITS == 0U)
+    {
       /* Disable the write protection for RTC registers */
       __HAL_RTC_WRITEPROTECTION_DISABLE(hrtc);
       
       /* Enter Initialization mode */
       status = RTC_EnterInitMode(hrtc);
       ...
+    }
}


With regards,

paragraphs: Issue #24: HAL_RTC_Init() looses time Title: HAL_RTC_Init() looses time

Body:
**Describe the set-up**
I'm using a STM32H743 on a custom board with 32768 Hz LSE crystal and battery-backed up RTC.

**Describe the bug**
Every time HAL_RTC_Init() is called, the RTC looses some sub-seconds.

HAL_RTC_Init should check if the RTC is already initializied (year != 0) and/or matches the desired configuration, and skip unnecessary stopping of the clock. According to the data sheet 45.3.8, the library should also check the INITS flag in the RTC_ISR register.
This is big a problem, because CubeMX5 will emit code that calls HAL_RTC_Init on each reset, without checking if it's already initialized. On each reset, you will loose up to 1s.

**How To Reproduce**
1. Read time with HAL_RTC_GetTime &HAL_RTC_GetDate and print to console
2. Call HAL_RTC_Init
3. Repeat
While doing this, the clock will gradually loose seconds.

title: [H7] #23: STM32CubeMX code generator is calling ethernetif_input instead of ethernet_link_thread.
content:
Issue #23: STM32CubeMX code generator is calling ethernetif_input instead of ethernet_link_thread.

Title: STM32CubeMX code generator is calling ethernetif_input instead of ethernet_link_thread.

Body:
**Describe the set-up**
* Hardware:  Proprietary hardware containing STM32H743 and external FLASH and external SDRAM.
* IDE:  VisualGDB with GCC 9.2.1
* FreeRTOS: V10.1.2
* CMSIS: V2.0
* Package:  STM32Cube FW_H7 V1.6.0

**Describe the bug**
When LWIP_NETIF_LINK_CALLBACK is enable in the STM32CubeMX LWIP middleware, it generates the following code from the FW_H7 V1.6.0 package:


/* Set the link callback function, this function is called on change of link status*/
  netif_set_link_callback(&gnetif, ethernet_link_status_updated);
 
  /* Create the Ethernet link handler thread */
  memset(&attributes, 0x0, sizeof(osThreadAttr_t));
  attributes.name = "EthLink";
  attributes.stack_size = INTERFACE_THREAD_STACK_SIZE;
  attributes.priority = osPriorityRealtime;
  osThreadNew(ethernetif_input, &gnetif, &attributes);


In the last line, it is starting a thread with an entry function of ethernetif_input.  However, that function has nothing to do with the ethernet link status.  Previous versions of this package used an entry function for the thread called ethernet_link_thread.

**How To Reproduce**
1. Indicate the global behavior of your application project
Proprietary application with Ethernet port and LWIP as the TCP/IP stack.

2. The modules that you suspect to be the cause of the problem (Driver, BSP, MW ...)
The **_MX_LWIP_Init()_** function in the file **_lwip.c_**.

3. The use case that generates the problem
See (4)

4. How we can reproduce the problem
Create an STM32CubeMX project that uses the FW_H7 V1.6.0 package and includes LWIP middleware with the LWIP_NETIF_LINK_CALLBACK option enabled. The code that initiates the thread that monitors the Ethernet link uses the wrong start function.


**Additional context**
This code in MX_LWIP_Init():
osThreadNew(ethernetif_input, &gnetif, &attributes);
should be changed to:
osThreadNew(ethernet_link_thread, &gnetif, &attributes);

**Screenshots**
If applicable, add screenshots to help explain your problem.

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-02-05T08:27:21Z]
Hi David,

First, allow me to thank you for all the points you are raising through these issues. However, aspects related to Cube MX tool are not handled at GitHub level. They are rather treated at **ST Community** level, precisely within the [Cube MX](https://community.st.com/s/topic/0TO0X000000BTr8WAG/stm32cubemx) dedicated section.

There, you may find posts that already raised the issue you are pointing out here. Otherwise, you can create a new post to submit your issue. Please, allow me to close this issue. Thank you for your comprehension.

With regards,

title: [H7] #22: Stalling at the DataOutStage in usbd_core.c leads to enumeration issues under Windows 10/7
paragraphs: Issue #22: Stalling at the DataOutStage in usbd_core.c leads to enumeration issues under Windows 10/7
Title: Stalling at the DataOutStage in usbd_core.c leads to enumeration issues under Windows 10/7

enumeration fails for a lot of users because of stalling of the endpoint during the usb configuration stage. The issue seems to occure with pretty much every STM32, I can confirm it for the STM32L4, STM32H7 and STM32F7 series. The effects can easily be seen by using a USB protocol analyzer like USBTrace. A quick fix is to remove the line USBD_LL_StallEP(pdev, 0U); completely which does not interfere with the USB functionality in any way. The folowing thread describes the bug in more detail where I also recently posted the solution: https://community.st.com/s/question/0D50X00009XkiIASAZ/stm32f405rg-vcp-error-this-device-cannot-startcode-10

Kind regards fstap Comments:

Thank you for having reported this issue.

This issue may already have been detected and fixed internally. If it is the case, the fix may already be available in some firmware Cube package recently published. If not, we will make a fix available soon, hopefully.
Please allow me to introduce @bouattay who will comment your post and provide you with more details about this point and thank you once more for your contribution.

title: [H7] #21: SDMMC: Hard-coded clock dividers only work for 200MHz MMCCLK
content:
Issue #21: SDMMC: Hard-coded clock dividers only work for 200MHz MMCCLK

Title: SDMMC: Hard-coded clock dividers only work for 200MHz MMCCLK

Body:
For MMC and SD-Card operation, the clock dividers are hard-coded and work for 200 MHz sdmmc_clk _only_:

https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/3d3ffbe6e15fc7e4d8a4d275e9fb3a334e31c8a3/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_sdmmc.h#L697-L704

CubeMX5 allows to configure the SDMMC peripheral with up to 480 MHz, so the clocks will be much to fast (e.g. 960 kHz during init instead of 400 kHz).

-> The dividers should be re-calculated according to the peripheral clock in  stm32h7xx_hal_sd.c and stm32h7xx_hal_mmc.c.

(stm32h7xx_hal_sd.c already uses HAL_RCCEx_GetPeriphCLKFreq(RCC_PERIPHCLK_SDMMC) to calculate some delays. This must also be done for the divisors).

Comments:
[ASELSTM - 2020-02-03T10:53:48Z]
Hi thomask77,

The issue you pointed out has been communicated to our development teams. It will be investigated soon. We will be back to you as soon as we get an answer. Thank you for your patience.

With regards,

[ASELSTM - 2020-02-14T10:23:39Z]
Hi thomask77,

Our development team  has confirmed the issue you pointed out and here is their reply :

The clock divider is configured through the init structure to allow the user setting the desired clock frequency. So, the user could use either the define provided in the .h file or define his own dividers.
But since the high speed and ultra-high speed features have been implemented, these define are also used in the driver. Then, as indicated in the issue, to have the appropriate clock frequency when not running at max frequency, it should be necessary to calculate the correct divider and not use directly the define.

Since the issue is confirmed, a fix will be implemented and made available in future release. Thank you once again for pointing out the issue and for your active contribution.

With regards,

[ASELSTM - 2020-02-14T10:24:06Z]
ST Internal Reference: 80503

[ASELSTM - 2021-03-09T13:43:15Z]
Hi @thomask77,

I hope you are fine. The issue you reported has been fixed in the frame of version v1.9.0 of the STM32CubeH7 published recently on GitHub.

Thank you again for having reported.

With regards,

[davhak - 2021-10-16T11:13:27Z]
Sorry if this is not correct but i do not see any difference in content of (stm32h7xx_ll_sdmmc.h) of version v1.9.0 and of the same file in v.1.8.3. Basically, all the HAL Driver files in version v1.9.0 are identical to previous version v1.8.3. So it is not clear what has been fixed in terms of "SDMMC_INIT_CLK_DIV" macro. In STM32H& the M4 core can be as high as 240 MHz. and the divider value of 0xFA still needs to be a different number.

title: [H7] #20: The HAL_NOR_ProgramBuffer() has a few defects
content:
Issue #20: The HAL_NOR_ProgramBuffer() has a few defects

Title: The HAL_NOR_ProgramBuffer() has a few defects

Body:
**Describe the set-up**
Hardware: Proprietary hardware containing STM32H743 and external FLASH and external SDRAM.
IDE: VisualGDB with GCC 9.2.1

**Describe the bug**
1.  The calculation for *p_endaddress* is incorrect.  It adds *uwBufferSize* to *uwAddress* to create the pointer, but *uwBufferSize* is the word count, not the byte count.  Therefore *uwBufferSize* should be multiplied by 2 to give the byte count.  Alternatively, *uwAddress* could be turned into a word pointer prior to adding *uwBufferSize* to it.
2. The local variables *p_currentaddress* and *lastloadedaddress* are used incorrectly in some parts of the code.  The variables contain the **internal** address of the FLASH, but they are sometimes used as if they are offsets from the device location.  When they are used as pointers to the FLASH they should have the FLASH device address added to them beforehand.

**How To Reproduce**
1. Indicate the global behavior of your application project
The bootloader is writing an application to external FLASH.

2. The modules that you suspect to be the cause of the problem (Driver, BSP, MW ...)
The *HAL_NOR_ProgramBuffer()* function in the file **stm32h7xx_hal_nor.c**

3. The use case that generates the problem
Write a buffer to the external NOR FLASH.

4. How we can reproduce the problem
Call it.

**Additional context**
The whole function needs to be cleaned up.  I would replace *p_endaddress* with a count down counter.  However, if you are just interested in fixing it:

The code:

    p_endaddress      = (const uint16_t *)(uwAddress + (uwBufferSize - 1U));
Should be changed to:

    p_endaddress      = (const uint16_t *)(uwAddress + (2 * (uwBufferSize - 1U)));
or alternatively

    p_endaddress      = ((const uint16_t *)uwAddress) + (uwBufferSize - 1U);

Later in the function, the code:

    while (p_currentaddress <= p_endaddress)
    {
      /* Store last loaded address & data value (for polling) */
      lastloadedaddress = (uint32_t)p_currentaddress;

      NOR_WRITE(p_currentaddress, *data);

      data++;
      p_currentaddress ++;
    }

    NOR_WRITE((uint32_t)(lastloadedaddress), NOR_CMD_DATA_BUFFER_AND_PROG_CONFIRM);

Should be changed to:

    while (p_currentaddress <= p_endaddress)
    {
      /* Store last loaded address & data value (for polling) */
      lastloadedaddress = (uint32_t)p_currentaddress;

      NOR_WRITE(deviceaddress + (uint32_t)p_currentaddress, *data);

      data++;
      p_currentaddress ++;
    }

      NOR_WRITE((uint32_t)(deviceaddress + lastloadedaddress), NOR_CMD_DATA_BUFFER_AND_PROG_CONFIRM);


**Screenshots**
None

Comments:
[ASELSTM - 2020-02-03T11:07:54Z]
Hi David,

Thank you for reporting this issue. It will be forwarded to our development teams for analysis. We will be back to you as soon as we get an answer. Thank you in advance for your patience.

With regards,

[ASELSTM - 2020-02-04T15:35:42Z]
Hi David, 

As promised i have reported your remarks to our development teams and they confirmed the issue. A fix will be implemented and made available in future release. Thank you once again for your contribution.

With regards,

[ASELSTM - 2020-02-04T15:36:20Z]
ST Internal Reference: 80491

[AAganichev - 2020-08-04T17:24:54Z]
Hi!

I'd suggest to unify API for the whole set of HAL_NOR_* functions. I'd say HAL_NOR_Read and HAL_NOR_Write should accept uwAddress relative to base address of Flash instead of absolute pointer pAddress.

And there's another issue with the CubeMX: if you select different FMC bank mappings the addresses are not changed respectively. It would be nice to be fixed as well.

Should I report these issues separately or they can be done within same issue?

Regards,
Alexander

1.  The calculation for *p_endaddress* is incorrect.  It adds *uwBufferSize* to *uwAddress* to create the pointer, but *uwBufferSize* is the word count, not the byte count.  Therefore *uwBufferSize* should be multiplied by 2 to give the byte count.  Alternatively, *uwAddress* could be turned into a word pointer prior to adding *uwBufferSize* to it.
2. The local variables *p_currentaddress* and *lastloadedaddress* are used incorrectly in some parts of the code.  The variables contain the **internal** address of the FLASH, but they are sometimes used as if they are offsets from the device location.  When they are used as pointers to the FLASH they should have the FLASH device address added to them beforehand.

**How To Reproduce**
1. Indicate the global behavior of your application project
The bootloader is writing an application to external FLASH.

2. The modules that you suspect to be the cause of the problem (Driver, BSP, MW ...)
The *HAL_NOR_ProgramBuffer()* function in the file **stm32h7xx_hal_nor.c**

3. The use case that generates the problem
Write a buffer to the external NOR FLASH.

4. How we can reproduce the problem
Call it.

**Additional context**
The whole function needs to be cleaned up.  I would replace *p_endaddress* with a count down counter.  However, if you are just interested in fixing it:

p_endaddress      = (const uint16_t *)(uwAddress + (uwBufferSize - 1U));
Should be changed to:

p_endaddress      = (const uint16_t *)(uwAddress + (2 * (uwBufferSize - 1U)));
or alternatively
p_endaddress      = ((const uint16_t *)uwAddress) + (uwBufferSize - 1U);
NOR_WRITE((uint32_t)(lastloadedaddress), NOR_CMD_DATA_BUFFER_AND_PROG_CONFIRM);
NOR_WRITE(deviceaddress + (uint32_t)p_currentaddress, *data);
NOR_WRITE((uint32_t)(deviceaddress + lastloadedaddress), NOR_CMD_DATA_BUFFER_AND_PROG_CONFIRM);

**Screenshots**
None Comments:
[ASELSTM - 2020-02-03T11:07:54Z]
Hi David,
Thank you for reporting this issue. It will be forwarded to our development teams for analysis. We will be back to you as soon as we get an answer. Thank you in advance for your patience.

With regards, [ASELSTM - 2020-02-04T15:35:42Z]
Hi David,

As promised i have reported your remarks to our development teams and they confirmed the issue. A fix will be implemented and made available in future release. Thank you once again for your contribution.

With regards, [ASELSTM - 2020-02-04T15:36:20Z]
ST Internal Reference: 80491

I'd suggest to unify API for the whole set of HAL_NOR_* functions. I'd say HAL_NOR_Read and HAL_NOR_Write should accept uwAddress relative to base address of Flash instead of absolute pointer pAddress.
And there's another issue with the CubeMX: if you select different FMC bank mappings the addresses are not changed respectively. It would be nice to be fixed as well.
Should I report these issues separately or they can be done within same issue?

Regards,
Alexander [ASELSTM - 2021-03-09T13:45:31Z]
Hi @david-oz00,
I hope you are fine. The issue you reported has been fixed in the frame of version v1.9.0 of the STM32CubeH7 published recently on GitHub.
Thank you again for having reported. With regards,
issue_title: The HAL_NOR_ProgramBuffer() has a few defects

title: [H7] #19: __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET() sometime causes microprocessor to reset
content:
Issue #19: __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET() sometime causes microprocessor to reset

Title: __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET() sometime causes microprocessor to reset

Body:
**Setup**
Hardware: Proprietary hardware containing STM32H743 and external FLASH and external SDRAM.
IDE: VisualGDB with GCC 9.2.1

**Bug Description**
When the HAL_DeInit() function calls the __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET() function the microprocessor sometimes resets.

    #define __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET()          (RCC->AHB3RSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)

**How To Reproduce**
1. Indicate the global behavior of your application project
A small FLASH programmer is running in internal RAM (DTCMRAM @ 0x20000000).  After it programs the external flash it unloads itself and calls HAL_DeInit() which calls __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET().  At this point the microprocessor resets.

2. The modules that you suspect to be the cause of the problem (Driver, BSP, MW ...)
The __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET() resides in stm32h7xx_hal_rcc.h

3. The use case that generates the problem
Calling the HAL_DeInit() when the program prepares to unload itself.

4. How we can reproduce the problem
I don't know all conditions which makes the function fail.  I suspect it has something to do with calling the function from internal RAM.  I don't think you need to reproduce the problem.  It is clearly a bug (see below) which should be rectified.


**Additional context**
The code writes all 1's to the AHB3RSTR register.  However, the STM32H7 reference manual says the reserved bits in that register must remain at the reset value (i.e. 0's)

This code:

    #define __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET()          (RCC->AHB3RSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)

should be replaced by:

    #define __HAL_RCC_AHB3_RESET_ALL               (RCC_AHB3RSTR_MDMARST |       \
                                                    RCC_AHB3RSTR_DMA2DRST |      \
                                                    RCC_AHB3RSTR_JPGDECRST |     \
                                                    RCC_AHB3RSTR_FMCRST |        \
                                                    RCC_AHB3RSTR_QSPIRST |       \
                                                    RCC_AHB3RSTR_SDMMC1RST)

    #define __HAL_RCC_AHB3_FORCE_RESET()          (RCC->AHB3RSTR = __HAL_RCC_AHB3_RESET_ALL)    

**Screenshots**
None

Comments:
[david-oz00 - 2020-01-24T02:51:51Z]
I've noticed similar functions incorrectly write all 1's to the registers.  E.g

    #define __HAL_RCC_AHB1_FORCE_RESET()             (RCC->AHB1RSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)
    #define __HAL_RCC_AHB2_FORCE_RESET()           (RCC->AHB2RSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)
    #define __HAL_RCC_APB3_FORCE_RESET()         (RCC->APB3RSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)
    #define __HAL_RCC_APB1L_FORCE_RESET()        (RCC->APB1LRSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)
    #define __HAL_RCC_APB1H_FORCE_RESET()        (RCC->APB1HRSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)
    #define __HAL_RCC_APB2_FORCE_RESET()         (RCC->APB2RSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)
    #define __HAL_RCC_APB4_FORCE_RESET()           (RCC->APB4RSTR = 0xFFFFFFFFU)

I haven't seen any detrimental affects from these functions, unlike the one described in the initial report.  However, technically the HAL should not be writing 1's to the reserved bits of these registers.

[ALABSTM - 2020-02-03T13:51:25Z]
Hi David,

Thank you for this report. May I ask you the STM32CubeH7 firmware revision you have used to get this problem?

With regards,

[david-oz00 - 2020-02-04T01:38:26Z]
Hi ALABSTM,
The package I am using is STM32Cube FW_H7 V1.6.0.  I've also attached an excerpt from the stm32H7 reference manual stating the reserve bits should not change.

![stm32h7_rm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/60245891/73705840-edfd6e00-4731-11ea-9a75-90ad36e328bd.jpg)

[ALABSTM - 2020-02-04T09:25:52Z]
Hi David,

May I ask you why you have closed this issue? It seems to be relevant.

With regards,

[david-oz00 - 2020-02-04T09:28:44Z]
I was about to ask you the same thing :)  I think it is relevant too.  Can you re-open it?

[david-oz00 - 2020-02-04T09:30:14Z]
I must have hit the close button by mistake.  Sorry.

[ALABSTM - 2020-02-04T09:31:34Z]
Hi David,

Done! I will forward it to our development teams. I will be back to you as soon as I get an answer.

With regards,

[ALABSTM - 2020-02-04T09:55:25Z]
Hi David,

Please allow me to introduce @BJASTM from our development teams who can better answer your question.

With regards,

[ALABSTM - 2020-02-07T13:03:57Z]
Hi David,

Our development teams confirmed the issue you have pointed out. This will be fixed and made available in a future firmware package release. Thank you once more for your contribution.

With regards,

**PS:** Below one of the faulty macros to be fixed.
https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/3d3ffbe6e15fc7e4d8a4d275e9fb3a334e31c8a3/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_hal_rcc.h#L4531

[ALABSTM - 2020-02-07T13:16:33Z]
ST Internal Reference: 80761

[vs4dhak - 2020-05-19T00:26:21Z]
@ALABSTM, I see that this is on the "To Release" section on the internal bug tracker on the development board. Is this available now? Are there temporary workarounds?

[ALABSTM - 2020-05-26T05:03:13Z]
Hi @veda-s4dhak,

The fix has been internally implemented but not is released yet. It is planned to be part of the **v1.8.0** firmware package release. We cannot share a date for the moment unfortunately, but we hope it will be soon enough.

In the meanwhile, please find attached [this](https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/files/4679968/stm32h7xx_hal_rcc.zip) patch with the updated stm32h7xx_hal_rcc.h file.

With regards,

title: [H7] #18: Invalid HAL version number in stm32h7xx_hal.c file
content:
Issue #18: Invalid HAL version number in stm32h7xx_hal.c file

Title: Invalid HAL version number in stm32h7xx_hal.c file

Body:
There is an invalid HAL version number in stm32h7xx_hal.c file.
HAL version 1.6.0 is reported as 1.7.0
https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/3d3ffbe6e15fc7e4d8a4d275e9fb3a334e31c8a3/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Src/stm32h7xx_hal.c#L53

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-02-04T09:52:11Z]
Hi Jiří,

Actually, STM32CubeH7 firmware package v1.6.0 includes HAL package v1.7.0, as indicated within the **Release_Notes.html** file.

Please allow me to introduce @BJASTM from our development teams who can better answer your question.

With regards,

[engycz - 2020-02-04T09:58:09Z]
It's confusing that the numbering differs, isn't it?

[ALABSTM - 2020-03-30T15:41:48Z]
Hi Jiří,

I asked our development teams for an explanation about this gap between the firmware's and the HAL's versions, particularly as the HAL's precedes the firmware's.

This gap started from the STM32CubeH7 **v1.4.0** published on the 5th of April 2019. In the frame of this publication, CMSIS Device and HAL components were already versioned v1.5.0 both.

Actually, at some point in the past, our development teams have been requested to deliver a major release of CMSIS Device and HAL components **outside the frame of** a firmware package. This has been done on the 30th of November 2018 when version **v1.4.0** of both components has been released.

To summarize, we have:
* CMSIS D + HAL v1.3.0 --> FW v1.3.0
* CMSIS D + HAL v1.4.0 (released as **standalone** components)
* CMSIS D + HAL v1.5.0 --> FW v1.4.0
* CMSIS D + HAL v1.6.0 --> FW v1.5.0
* CMSIS D + HAL v1.7.0 --> FW v1.6.0
* CMSIS D + HAL v1.8.0 --> FW v1.7.0

Below are links to the different release notes in case you would like to have an overview of the different publication dates:
* Release note of the STM32CubeH7 FW package ([link](https://htmlpreview.github.io/?https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Release_Notes.html))
* Release note of the H7 CMSIS Device component ([link](https://htmlpreview.github.io/?https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Drivers/CMSIS/Device/ST/STM32H7xx/Release_Notes.html))
* Release note of the H7 HAL driver component ([link](https://htmlpreview.github.io/?https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/master/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Release_Notes.html))

I hope this answers your question and excuse the delayed reply.

With regards,

**PS:** You may notice that in the firmware's release note, version 1.7.0 of the firmware is still reported to integrate version v1.7.0 of both CMSIS Device and HAL components. This is simply a typo that should be corrected soon hopefully.

[ALABSTM - 2020-04-02T11:16:18Z]
Hi Jiří,

I hope you find in my last comment a satisfying answer to your question. Please allow me to close this issue now.

With regards,

title: [H7] #17: Bug in USB implementation
paragraphs: Issue #17: Bug in USB implementation Title: Bug in USB implementation

Body:
Hello.
I have found a bug in USB implementation in STM32CubeH7 (1.4.0 - 1.6.0). Steps to reproduce:
1) Open the project from STM32H743I-EVAL\Applications\USB_Device\MSC_Standalone\MDK-ARM directory
2) Apply HEAP fix from https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/pull/16
3) Build the project and download it to STM32H743I-EVAL board
4) Wait for STM driver letter to appear
5) Copy a file with approx size 20MB to STM drive
6) Start copying above file from STM drive
7) In the middle of copying unplug the USB cable
8) Plug USB cable back
9) From this time USB is not recognized by the operating system anymore 
![obrazek](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6651271/72711888-42b5bc00-3b6a-11ea-9b4a-e4a94571f7fd.png)
The issue is not present in STM32CubeH7 1.3.2

Comments:
[ALABSTM - 2020-02-03T11:52:14Z]
Hi Engycz,
Please allow me to introduce @bouattay from our USB development teams. Thank you for having reported this point.

[engycz - 2020-02-04T09:22:44Z]
The proposed change from ST Case Number 00097303 to stm32h7xx_ll_usb.c fixes this issue
The fix is internally implemented and should be visible for H7 family starting from Cube Release v1.8.0
issue_title: Bug in USB implementation

title: [H7] #15: register storage class specifier is incompatible with C++17
content:
Issue #15: register storage class specifier is incompatible with C++17

Title: register storage class specifier is incompatible with C++17

Body:
Hi!

The register storage class specifier was deprecated in C++11 and removed in C++17.

There are a few register keywords in some inline functions in stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h:

https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/e261d820b398846a94f22f4aeb32d86c29546efb/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h#L2264

So, if I include the stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h header file from C++, gcc will complain:

c++
Libraries/STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.5.0/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h: In function 'uint32_t LL_ADC_DMA_GetRegAddr(ADC_TypeDef*, uint32_t)':                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
Libraries/STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.5.0/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h:2264:21: warning: ISO C++17 does not allow 'register' storage class specifier [-Wregister]                                                                                                                                                                                                             
 2264 |   register uint32_t data_reg_addr;                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
      |                     ^~~~~~~~~~~~~                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
Libraries/STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.5.0/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h: In function 'void LL_ADC_SetCalibrationLinearFactor(ADC_TypeDef*, uint32_t, uint32_t)':                                                                                                                                                                                                               
Libraries/STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.5.0/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h:2517:21: warning: ISO C++17 does not allow 'register' storage class specifier [-Wregister]                                                                                                                                                                                                             
 2517 |   register uint32_t timeout_cpu_cycles = ADC_LINEARITY_BIT_TOGGLE_TIMEOUT;                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
      |                     ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
Libraries/STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.5.0/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h: In function 'uint32_t LL_ADC_GetCalibrationLinearFactor(ADC_TypeDef*, uint32_t)':                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
Libraries/STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.5.0/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h:2544:21: warning: ISO C++17 does not allow 'register' storage class specifier [-Wregister]                                                                                                                                                                                                             
 2544 |   register uint32_t timeout_cpu_cycles = ADC_LINEARITY_BIT_TOGGLE_TIMEOUT;

The same is possibly true for other header files, also in HAL code for other STM32 CPUs, for example here:

https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeF4/blob/master/Drivers/STM32F4xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32f4xx_ll_adc.h#L1828

I think, the keyword should just be removed.

[ASELSTM - 2020-04-02T11:29:26Z]
ST Internal Reference: 83751
paragraphs: Issue #15: register storage class specifier is incompatible with C++17

Title: register storage class specifier is incompatible with C++17 Body:
Hi!
The register storage class specifier was deprecated in C++11 and removed in C++17.
There are a few register keywords in some inline functions in stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h:
https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/e261d820b398846a94f22f4aeb32d86c29546efb/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Inc/stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h#L2264
So, if I include the stm32h7xx_ll_adc.h header file from C++, gcc will complain:

c++

 2264 |   register uint32_t data_reg_addr;

title: [H7] #14: Regression in QSPI vs. v1.3.0
content:
Issue #14: Regression in QSPI vs. v1.3.0

Title: Regression in QSPI vs. v1.3.0

Body:
Hi!

**Describe the set-up**

We are using an STM32H743XIH6 (Rev. V) @ 480 MHz, on a custom board with a W25Q128JVEIQ flash in Quad-SPI mode. Our Toolchain is gcc-arm-none-eabi-9-2019-q4-major.

**Describe the bug**

We're using the HAL_QSPI_Transmit_DMA function.

When using CubeMX libraries v1.3.0, we could run QSPI with 120 MHz clock (Clock Prescaler 1).

After an upgrade to CubeMX libraries v1.5.0, we had to set the prescaler to 8, or even 16 to achieve stable operation, otherwise, HAL_QSPI_Transmit_DMA would fail. 

We can send you the CubeMX file if required.

**Additional context**

I haven't looked into the bug in detail yet, but I could narrow it down to this single change:

v1.5.0, **bad**:

https://github.com/STMicroelectronics/STM32CubeH7/blob/e261d820b398846a94f22f4aeb32d86c29546efb/Drivers/STM32H7xx_HAL_Driver/Src/stm32h7xx_hal_qspi.c#L1333-L1334

v1.3.0, **good**:
c
      __HAL_QSPI_ENABLE_IT(hqspi, QSPI_IT_TE);


After reverting the change, we can run with 120 MHz again.

What was the reason for the change in 1.5.0?
Perhaps you can already try to reproduce on an evaluation board.

With regards,

Our development teams confirmed the issue you pointed out. A fix will be implemented and made available in future release. Thank you once again for your contribution.

With regards,

title: [H7] #13: DSI/emWin crash on dual-core H7
paragraphs: Issue #13: DSI/emWin crash on dual-core H7

Title: DSI/emWin crash on dual-core H7 Body:
Hello,

example affected:
/Projects/STM32H747I-DISCO/Applications/STemWin/STemWin_HelloWorld/CM7/STemWin/App/BASIC_HelloWorld.c
running on non-modified STM32H747I-DISCO board.

Without edit to this file, 'Hello world!' string is displayed on the screen, but there is very limited amount of time that the function can print to the LCD, after that i suspect a crash of the emWin or the DSI LCD driver.

for(i = 0; i < 0x5FFFFF; i++)
    	__asm(".hword 0x46C0");
GUI_DispStringAt("Hello world!", (LCD_GetXSize()-100)/2, (LCD_GetYSize()-20)/2 + 40);

decreasing the delay to 0x5FFFF allows for the second line to be printed. So anything printed before that timeout is reached will be displayed, but nothing after that. The four LEDs that get toggled by the timer will still blink.

I have similar issue with other example projects (the Menu Launcher, the STemWin Demonstration)
that use similar version of the same DSI driver.

- some type of watchdog running by default ?
- the M4 crashes maybe and indirectly affects the LCD print ?
- some unknown emWin library crash (i have used it on various ST and NXP chips and never seen this issue before).

Comments:
[m0nka - 2020-01-11T09:10:52Z]
Further to this i have followed the STemWin544.pdf, page 54 and added this instead of my code:
It would only count to 0468 and then stop refresh the screen. But the loop is still running, for example if i add BSP_LED_Toggle(LED4) inside;

[ASELSTM - 2020-01-15T14:20:20Z]
Hi Krassi,
This issue has been forwarded to our development teams. We will be back to you as soon as they provide us with feedback. Thank you for your contribution.

With regards, [m0nka - 2020-01-18T12:42:10Z]
Thank you!

So far i have been able to make only one of the examples to work for me (Menu Launcher), but with keeping it using the emWin Window Manager exclusively and monitoring the two interrupts toggle the onboard LEDs.

I see that LTDC interrupt is enabled in most of the emWin examples, but doesn't fire, only the DSI and DMA2D. So i keep the two LEDs blinking. When any of the above mentioned Refresh blockage happens, the LEDs will stop blink (i have also scope connected there, as it is a bit faster for the eye).
You can close the issue. Not sure if bug or feature, but apparently in 5.44 you can not use the 2D drawing calls directly.
WM_SetCallback(WM_HBKWIN, _cbBk); needs to be created and in WM_PAINT event only the normal 2D calls could be issued (while WM_InvalidateWindow(WM_HBKWIN); in the main task loop).
Thanks for everyone who spent time on this. BR.
issue_title: DSI/emWin crash on dual-core H7

title: [H7] #12: USB MSC Stack: Can't enumerate when disk is not ready
content:
Issue #12: USB MSC Stack: Can't enumerate when disk is not ready

Title: USB MSC Stack: Can't enumerate when disk is not ready

Body:
**Describe the set-up**
 * The board: STM32H747I-EVAL
 * IDE: Iar 8.30

**Describe the bug**
 * Windows can't enumerate device when SD card is not ready

**How To Reproduce**
 * Suspected modules: USB Device Stack, MSC class
 * The use case that generates the problem: SD Card is not inserted
 * How we can reproduce the problem:
   * Remove sd card from sd slot
   * Run STM32Cube_FW_H7_V1.5.0\Projects\STM32H747I-EVAL\Applications\USB_Device\MSC_Standalone\EWARM\USBD_MSC.eww
   * Plug USB to Windows PC

**Additional context**
* The returning value in int8_t SCSI_ProcessCmd(USBD_HandleTypeDef *pdev, uint8_t lun, uint8_t *cmd) is not handled
* Ref #11 for the fix.

Comments:

Hi Canhkha,

Thank you for your message. Please allow me to introduce @bouattay from our development teams. He will analyze the issue you described and the relevance of the solution you suggested.

Please note I took the initiative to remove a couple of lines from your original description as these lines are only intended to serve as a guideline for users to state their issues. Removing them increases the readability of your message.

With regards,

[ALABSTM - 2020-01-06T10:00:17Z]
Hi Canhkha,

As already said by @bouattay, "Many changes have been added in our latest device lib v2.6.0 including a similar fix to what you have shared".

Hopefully, a new revision of the STM32CubeH7 will be published soon which will include the fix. Thank you for your patience and for your contribution once again.

With regards,
